title: Sj gren s Disease
authors:
Haralampos M. Moutsopoulos,

Clio P. Mavragani

items.subtopic: Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Understanding Sjögren's Disease Prevalence and Demographics
items.content: Sjögren's disease is an autoimmune condition primarily affecting middle-aged women with a female-to-male ratio of 10–20:1. It can occur at any age, including childhood, but earlier onset correlates with more aggressive systemic manifestations.
keyPoints: Prevalence of Sjögren's disease is ~0.5–1%.
5–20% of patients with other autoimmune diseases may exhibit sicca symptoms.
Middle-aged women are primarily affected (female-to-male ratio 10–20:1).
Earlier onset correlates with more aggressive systemic manifestations.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Pathogenesis of Sjögren's Disease: Key Mechanisms and Risk Factors
items.content: The pathogenesis involves lymphocytic infiltration of exocrine glands and B-cell hyperreactivity. Genetic factors such as HLA DQA1 *0501, IRF5, STAT4, BAFF, EBF1, BLK, and complement pathway deficiencies contribute to disease susceptibility.
keyPoints: Lymphocytic infiltration primarily affects salivary and lachrymal glands.
B-cell hyperreactivity manifests as hypergammaglobulinemia and serum autoantibodies (e.g., rheumatoid factors, Ro52, Ro60, La).
Risk genetic loci include HLA DQA1 *0501, IRF5, STAT4, BAFF, EBF1, BLK, and complement pathway deficiencies.
Glandular epithelial cells express costimulatory molecules (e.g., Ro, La), proinflammatory cytokines, chemokines, TLRs 3, 7, 9, ICAM, CD40, and BAFF.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Lymphoma Risk in Sjögren's Disease: Pathogenetic Insights
items.content: Patients with Sjögren's disease have an increased risk of lymphoma development due to factors like inflammasome activation and elevated circulating BAFF, which contributes to B-cell survival.
keyPoints: Increased risk for lymphoma in Sjögren's disease patients.
Inflammasome activation is associated with lymphoma risk.
Circulating BAFF levels are elevated in patients, especially those with hypergammaglobulinemia and serum autoantibodies.
BAFF has an antiapoptotic effect on B lymphocytes.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Oral Manifestations in Sjögren's Disease
items.content: Sjögren's disease primarily affects exocrine glands, leading to symptoms such as dry mouth and difficulty swallowing. Oral examination may reveal a dry, erythematous mucosa with atrophic filiform papillae.
keyPoints: Principal oral symptom is xerostomia (dryness).
Symptoms include difficulty swallowing dry food, burning sensation in the mouth, increased dental caries, and problems wearing dentures.
Physical examination shows a dry, erythematous sticky oral mucosa with atrophic filiform papillae on the tongue dorsum.
Saliva from major glands is either not expressible or cloudy.
Enlargement of parotid or other major salivary glands occurs in two-thirds of patients.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Ocular Manifestations and Diagnostic Evaluation
items.content: Patients with Sjögren's disease often experience ocular symptoms due to keratoconjunctivitis sicca, characterized by dryness of the cornea and conjunctiva.
keyPoints: Common ocular symptoms include sandy or gritty feeling under eyelids, burning, secretions in thick strands at inner canthi, decreased tearing, redness, itching, eye fatigue, and increased photosensitivity.

Diagnostic evaluation includes Schirmer's I test for tear flow measurement, tear breakup time or lysozyme content determination, and slit-lamp examination with lissamine green or Rose Bengal staining to reveal punctuate corneal and bulbar conjunctival ulcerations.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Systemic Manifestations and Lymphoma Risk
items.content: Sjögren's disease can involve systemic manifestations, with a significant risk of lymphoma development among patients.
keyPoints: Extraglandular manifestations occur in one-third of patients, including fatigability, low-grade fever, Raynaud's phenomenon, myalgias, arthralgias, and nonerosive arthritis.
Lung involvement includes dry cough and rarely dyspnea due to bronchitis sicca and interstitial pneumonitis.
Renal involvement may present as interstitial nephritis with hyposthenuria and renal tubular dysfunction.
Sjögren's disease has the highest risk for lymphoma among autoimmune diseases, often presenting as extranodal low-grade marginal zone B-cell lymphomas.
Risk factors for lymphoma include B symptoms, lymph node mass >7 cm, and high or intermediate histologic grade.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Sjögren's Disease
items.title: Recognizing Autoantibodies in Sjögren's Disease
items.content: Autoantibodies are crucial for diagnosing Sjögren's disease and can indicate specific organ involvement. Elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, hypergammaglobulinemia, antinuclear antibodies, rheumatoid factors, and antibodies against Ro52/Ro60 and La autoantigens are common findings in two-thirds of patients.
keyPoints: Autoantibodies such as anticentromere suggest limited scleroderma-like features.
Antimitochondrial antibodies indicate potential liver involvement with autoimmune cholangitis.
Autoantibodies to 21-hydroxylase are linked to adrenal insufficiency.
Anticalponin-3 antibodies correlate with peripheral neuropathies.
Anticitrullinated peptide antibodies are associated with arthritis in Sjögren's patients.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Sjögren's Disease

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Sjögren's Disease
items.title: Approach to Diagnosing and Treating Sjögren's Disease
items.content: Diagnosis of Sjögren's disease involves identifying symptoms like eye/mouth dryness, salivary gland enlargement, or systemic manifestations. Lab tests include specific autoantibodies, serology for chronic infections, and labial biopsy. Treatment focuses on symptom relief through secretory stimulation and substitution.
keyPoints: Suspect Sjögren's with symptoms like eye/mouth dryness, salivary gland enlargement, or systemic manifestations such as Raynaud's phenomenon.
Use ophthalmic preparations (e.g., hydroxypropyl methylcellulose) for tear replacement and cyclosporine drops if corneal ulcerations are present.
For xerostomia, use water and consider pilocarpine or cevimeline to stimulate secretions.
Hydroxychloroquine or methotrexate can be used for arthralgias/mild arthritis.
Systemic involvement may require glucocorticoids or rituximab.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Sjögren's Disease

items.subtopic: Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of Sjögren's disease?
options: Lymphocytic infiltration of exocrine glands resulting in xerostomia and dry eyes.
Primarily affects middle-aged men with a female-to-male ratio of 1:10.
Exclusively presents as an organ-specific disease without systemic involvement.
Has no association with other autoimmune diseases.
items.explanation: Sjögren's disease is characterized by lymphocytic infiltration of exocrine glands resulting in xerostomia and dry eyes.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: What is the prevalence of Sjögren's disease?
items.explanation: The prevalence of Sjögren's disease is approximately 0.5–1%.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which cells are primarily involved in the lymphocytic infiltration of exocrine glands in Sjögren's disease?
options: Activated T lymphocytes and B-cell populations.
items.explanation: The major infiltrating cells in the affected exocrine glands are activated T lymphocytes, with B-cell populations prevailing in extensive infiltrations.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which genetic locus is implicated in Sjögren's disease?
items.explanation: The HLA DQA1 *0501 allele is an established risk genetic locus implicated in Sjögren's disease.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.question: Which diagnostic test is used to measure tear flow in patients suspected of having keratoconjunctivitis sicca due to Sjögren's disease?
items.explanation: Schirmer's I test is used for measuring tear flow in keratoconjunctivitis sicca.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.question: What is the typical nature of arthritis seen in patients with primary Sjögren's disease?
items.explanation: Arthritis in primary Sjögren's disease is nonerosive.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is a common oral symptom of Sjögren's disease?
items.explanation: Xerostomia, or dryness, is a common oral symptom of Sjögren's disease.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.question: What type of lymphoma is most commonly associated with Sjögren's disease?
items.explanation: Most lymphomas in Sjögren's disease are low-grade, marginal zone B-cell lymphomas.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which autoantibodies are associated with the occurrence of peripheral neuropathies in Sjögren's disease?
items.explanation: Anticalponin-3 antibodies have been recently associated with the occurrence of peripheral neuropathies.

items.question: Which autoantibodies may indicate liver involvement in the form of autoimmune cholangitis in Sjögren's disease?
items.explanation: The presence of antimitochondrial antibodies may connote liver involvement in the form of autoimmune cholangitis.

items.question: Which autoantibodies are detected in two-thirds of patients with Sjögren's disease?
items.explanation: In two-thirds of patients, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, hypergammaglobulinemia, antinuclear antibodies, rheumatoid factors, and antibodies against Ro52/Ro60 and La autoantigens are detected.

items.statement: Sjögren's disease primarily affects middle-aged men.
items.explanation: Sjögren's disease primarily affects middle-aged women with a female-to-male ratio of 10–20:1.

items.subtopic: Sjögren's Disease | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE | PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Glandular epithelial cells in Sjögren's disease express intracellular autoantigens Ro and La.
items.explanation: Ductal and acinar epithelial cells express the intracellular autoantigens Ro and La, contributing to autoimmune injury.

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.statement: Renal involvement in Sjögren's disease can lead to nephrocalcinosis if untreated acidosis occurs.
items.explanation: Untreated acidosis from renal tubular dysfunction may result in nephrocalcinosis.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.statement: Central nervous system involvement is common in Sjögren's disease.
items.explanation: Central nervous system involvement is rarely recognized in Sjögren's disease.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Sjögren's Disease
items.statement: Autoantibodies to citrullinated peptides are seen in Sjögren's patients with arthritis.
items.explanation: Autoantibodies to citrullinated peptides are indeed observed in Sjögren's patients with arthritis.
items.reference: Sj gren s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Sjögren's Disease

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Sjögren's Disease
items.statement: Anticentromere antibodies are associated with a clinical picture similar to that of diffuse scleroderma in Sjögren's disease.
items.explanation: Anticentromere autoantibodies are present in Sjögren's patients with a clinical picture similar to limited scleroderma, not diffuse scleroderma.

items.assertion: Patients with earlier onset of Sjögren's disease express a more aggressive phenotype.
items.reason: This is due to a high occurrence of systemic manifestations and serum autoantibodies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: The prevalence of Sjögren's disease is 10–20%.
items.reason: This percentage represents patients with other autoimmune diseases expressing sicca manifestations.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the prevalence of Sjögren's disease is ~0.5–1%. The reason is true but does not relate to the incorrect assertion.
